era_context: Early 14th century: The Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean and Middle East; the Yuan Dynasty rules China under Temür Khan; the Avignon Papacy is established in southern France; the Kingdom of Scotland is consolidating independence under Robert I (the Bruce); the Ottoman Empire is in its infancy in Anatolia; the Mali Empire is at its height in West Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian Ocean trade networks

events.title: Arrest and Trial of the Knights Templar
events.description: On Friday, 13 October 1306, King Philip IV of France orchestrated the simultaneous arrest of Knights Templar across French territories. The order, founded nearly two centuries earlier as a military-monastic institution, was accused of heresy, idol worship, and financial corruption. The arrests were coordinated with papal cooperation and marked the beginning of systematic suppression of the order. The Templar trials would continue for years, resulting in executions and the eventual dissolution of the order by 1312. This event exemplifies the conflict between emerging centralized monarchy and international religious authority.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay (Grand Master of the Templars)

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles: Jean de Saint-Victor, Grandes Chroniques de France

events.certainty_note: The date of 13 October 1306 is well-attested in contemporary chronicles and papal records. The coordinated nature and scale of arrests are documented.

events.title: Death of Temür Khan (Khaisan)
events.description: Temür Khan (also known as Khaisan), the third Yuan emperor and grandson of Kublai Khan, died in February 1306 after a reign marked by administrative reforms and attempts to stabilize the Mongol-ruled Chinese state. His death triggered a succession crisis that would destabilize the Yuan Dynasty for decades. Temür had promoted Confucian scholarship and attempted to restore the civil service examination system, but his reign was also marked by factional conflicts among Mongol nobility. His death is a watershed moment in Yuan history, leading to the reign of his brother Ayurbarwada and subsequent fragmentation of imperial authority.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th century

events.certainty_note: The death of Temür Khan in 1306 is well-documented in Yuan official histories and court records. The date is secure.

events.title: Robert I (the Bruce) Crowned King of Scotland
events.description: Robert I, known as Robert the Bruce, was crowned King of Scotland in March 1306 at Scone, following his murder of John Comyn and his claim to the Scottish throne. This coronation was a pivotal moment in Scottish independence, though Robert's position was initially precarious—he was excommunicated by the Pope and faced English military pressure from Edward I. The coronation represented the consolidation of Scottish resistance to English overlordship and initiated a decade-long struggle that would culminate in Scottish victory at Bannockburn (1314). Robert's reign fundamentally reshaped the political map of Britain.
events.certainty_note: The coronation in March 1306 is attested in multiple contemporary sources, though some details of the ceremony are reconstructed from later accounts.

events.title: Osman I Establishes Ottoman Principality
events.description: Osman I (Osman Bey), founder of the Ottoman Dynasty, declared independence from the declining Seljuk Sultanate of Rum in 1306, establishing the Ottoman Beylik (principality) in northwestern Anatolia. This event marks the traditional founding date of the Ottoman Empire, though the process was gradual. Osman's principality was initially small and surrounded by other Turkish beyliks, but his military success against Byzantine territories and his strategic location near Constantinople would eventually make the Ottomans the dominant power in the region. The declaration of independence was formalized through military victories and the minting of coins in his name.
key_figures: Osman I (Osman Bey) (Founder of Ottoman Dynasty)

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles: Aşıkpaşazade, Menaqib-i Osman Bey
Numismatic evidence (coins minted in Osman's name)

events.certainty_note: The date 1306 is traditional in Ottoman historiography, but the actual process of independence was gradual. Some sources place the formal declaration slightly earlier or later. The year 1299 is also cited in some traditions. 1306 represents the conventional dating in most modern scholarship.

events.title: Clement V Moves Papal Seat to Avignon
events.description: Pope Clement V, under pressure from King Philip IV of France and facing instability in Rome, relocated the papal seat from Rome to Avignon in southern France. This move, formalized in 1309 but initiated in 1306, began the period known as the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377), during which seven successive popes resided in Avignon rather than Rome. The relocation was controversial and symbolized the increasing influence of French monarchy over papal affairs. It also contributed to the fragmentation of papal authority and the eventual Western Schism. The move reflected broader shifts in European political power toward centralized monarchies.
events.certainty_note: The relocation process began in 1306 and was formalized by 1309. The year 1306 marks the beginning of the transition.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional date of Osman I's declaration of independence (1306) is disputed by some scholars, who argue the process was more gradual and that the formal break from Seljuk authority occurred earlier (c. 1299) or later. The significance of the Templar arrests as a purely religious matter is complicated by modern scholarship emphasizing Philip IV's financial motivations and the political dimensions of the conflict. The 'Avignon Papacy' framing as a period of papal weakness is challenged by some historians who note that Avignon popes maintained considerable administrative and diplomatic authority.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1306 is rich for Western Europe (France, England, Scotland, Papacy) and moderate for the Islamic world and Yuan China. The sources are predominantly written by elites and reflect court perspectives. The Templar trials are extensively documented but heavily influenced by confessions extracted under duress, making the accuracy of specific charges difficult to assess. Ottoman sources for this period are largely retrospective, compiled centuries later, introducing legendary elements. The year represents a pivotal moment in multiple regions, but the significance attributed to events varies by historiographic tradition (Western, Islamic, Chinese, Ottoman).

graph_edges.note: Templar arrests led to formal dissolution at Council of Vienne
Temür Khan's death triggered succession crisis and Ayurbarwada's reign
Robert I's coronation initiated the struggle culminating in Bannockburn
Ottoman founding was first step toward eventual conquest of Constantinople
Both events reflect the power of centralized monarchy over traditional institutions
